grammar.title: Imperative ‘alégrense’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of reflexive alegrarse. Pronoun attaches to the end and forces the written accent (a-LE-gren-se → alégrense).

grammar.title: ‘Para mí no es molestia + infinitive’
grammar.detail: Spanish prefers an infinitive after impersonal expressions: ‘no es molestia volver a escribirles’. Closer to English ‘it’s no trouble to write…’ than to a finite clause.

expression.note: The keynote of Philippians — joy not in circumstances, but ‘en el Señor’. Paul repeats this exact phrase in 4:4.

vocab.meaning: to beware of, to watch out for

grammar.title: Triple imperative for emphasis
grammar.detail: ‘Cuídense… cuídense… cuídense…’ The repetition is rhetorical, hammering home a single warning. Like English ‘Watch out! Watch out! Watch out!’

expression.note: A sharp pun against the Judaizers who insisted on circumcision. Paul flips the OT image — they don’t mark the covenant, they ‘mutilate’ flesh.

grammar.title: ‘por medio de’
grammar.detail: ‘by means of, through’ — three-word preposition. The single-word ‘mediante’ is a more compact alternative.

grammar.title: ‘poner la confianza en’
grammar.detail: Fixed collocation: ‘to place one’s confidence in’. Negative version here: ‘no ponemos nuestra confianza en esfuerzos humanos’.

expression.note: Paul redefines the term: the real ‘circumcised people’ are those who worship by the Spirit, not those marked in the flesh.
